summary: This post is a collection of short updates covering four topics. Spolsky discusses using CityDesk's development build to edit his own blog ('eating your own dogfood'), reflects on how his widely-read interview advice has made candidates game his specific techniques, draws a fascinating parallel between email viruses and Darwinian evolution through memetics, and recommends Rick Chapman's software marketing book while noting some of its content is aging.
key_points: Dogfooding is most effective when the stakes are real — Spolsky runs CityDesk inside a debugger so crashes during writing force immediate bug fixes
Publishing interview advice at scale creates a feedback loop where candidates optimize for the specific signals you described, rendering those signals useless
Email viruses follow Darwinian evolution: they have a genetic code (message text), replication (sending), mutation (variations), and natural selection (convincing messages spread further)
Software marketing knowledge is undervalued — most developers underestimate how much is involved and need humility about the complexity of selling software
money_quotes: The term 'eating your own dogfood,' in the software industry, means using the code you're developing for your own daily needs: basically, being a user as well as a developer, so the user empathy that is the hallmark of good software comes automatically.

The neat part is that if I'm writing a long essay for the site and the application crashes, I have a chance to debug it right there and then and in fact if I haven't saved in a while I must debug it right there and then, otherwise I won't be able to save my work.
My guerrilla guide is so well read that my old trick of looking for people who write their }'s immediately after their {'s doesn't work any more.

Everyone who interviews at Fog Creek always carefully does that now, and then they sort of look at me to make sure I noticed that they wrote their } immediately after their {. Tip: That's not what I'm looking for any more.
The ones with the best fake letters, e.g., the ones that persuade the most people to open the attachment, will survive and reproduce. The ones that aren't very convincing die out.
Before you try to sell software, you have to at least sit down and read this book cover to cover, if only to gain the humility to realize how much is involved in marketing.
key_insight: The best software practices — dogfooding, interviewing, even understanding threats like email viruses — require genuine engagement rather than going through the motions, because gaming any signal destroys its value.
